key_points: Limited direct student feedback on dorms specifically
Commuter campus option exists in Manchester with scholarship
No major dorm complaints surfaced, but also little praise
key_quotes: I go to their commuter school which isn't too far from where I work
My kid got a handwritten letter. It was nice.

key_points: Known as a party school — explicitly called out by students
Traditional college social scene, described as welcoming
Smaller campus means tighter social circles
key_quotes: Academics are notoriously sol... and it is a party school
UNH is going to be a more traditional college setting/cross section of people
I have lots of friends that go there

key_points: No specific dining hall feedback in the data
New Haven CT food scene is a major positive nearby
Pizza culture in New Haven is genuinely celebrated by locals
key_quotes: New Haven has the best pizza
BAR also has good pizza
We do have the best pizza here

key_points: One student failing engineering cited ADHD struggles with no support mentioned
Pressure to switch majors without student consent noted by international applicants
No mental health resources were praised or even mentioned
key_quotes: I have ADHD. I workout. I work harder than most people I know. I do about 80 hours
the same thing happen to me I applied to cellular and molecular biology but they recommended me to join in Master of Health care administration
I don't know. Unfortunately, colleges do see mental illness or instability as a red flag

key_points: Scholarships exist but are modest — one international student received only $1,899
Out-of-state costs hit $52k/year without significant aid
Manchester campus Millyard Scholarship can drop costs to ~$800/year
key_quotes: I got admit from the University of New Haven and received dean scholarship of only 1899$
New Hampshire gave me a scholarship it would still be about 52000 a year
I got into the manchester campus with the Millyard Scholarship and i'll be paying 800$ a year

key_points: Academics described as 'notoriously solid' but not rigorous
Forensic science and criminal justice are known programs
Engineering and CS seen as weaker than peers like Northeastern
key_quotes: Academics are notoriously solid
UNH is just much weaker, especially in CS
I am failing MATLAB, physics and statics... I need a 2.0 to continue engineering

key_points: UNH proactively contacted counselors about student AP research — unusual move
School switched from RD to rolling mid-cycle, confusing deferred applicants
Budget problems and program cuts flagged by at least one commenter
key_quotes: UNH has like a 87% acceptance and i have like really solid stats... why would UNH go OUT OF THEIR WAY to EMAIL my counselor
UNH is having terrible budget problems and making cuts that are degrading the school
they just announced today they're switching from regular decision to rolling admissions. what does that mean for my application?

key_points: Durham campus is scenic, near coast and Portsmouth — genuinely liked
University of New Haven sits in West Haven CT, near a city with serious safety issues
New Haven CT has stark poverty surrounding Yale; crime is real and documented
key_quotes: Literally located on the Great Bay, right next to Portsmouth, which is arguably a much better city than Burlington
I live in New Haven, the complete disregard for the danger in this city that some of these kids display is appalling
at the hotel I was handed a map... 'don't go outside these lines. period.'

key_points: Campus placement concerns raised directly by international MS Finance admits
Criminal justice major seen as limiting for post-grad opportunities
CS and engineering grads better served by Northeastern or stronger programs

I heard that the criminal justice major does not open a lot of opportunities
UNH is just much weaker, especially in CS. Reconsider Northeastern.

key_points: In-state or with aid, UNH Durham is a genuine bargain
Out-of-state without aid, $52k/year is hard to justify over stronger schools
Manchester campus with Millyard Scholarship may be best value play
key_quotes: If I went to UNH I could likely get my master's in 3-4 years from an accelerated program
New Hampshire gave me a scholarship it would still be about 52000 a year

key_points: Friendly, traditional New England college town feel at Durham
University of New Haven has a grittier urban reality than brochures suggest
Students who visit Durham tend to like the vibe; few who commit regret it
key_quotes: I loved it when I visited. NH is nice too
I strongly feel that I'll have a more enjoyable and traditional college experience at UNH
It is a great school with many government contracts, in a cool little town

Accelerated BS/MS program lets motivated students finish master's in 3-4 years total
Portsmouth NH nearby is a genuinely excellent small city, often overlooked
